title: Chow Rings, Decomposition of the Diagonal and the Topology of Families
speaker: Claire Voisin
affiliation: CNRS, Institut de Mathematiques de Jussieu, Paris
abstract: Lecture 2: On the Generalized Bloch and Hodge Conjectures for Complete Intersections

proof_stages.label: Chow-trivial complete intersections and vanishing cohomology
proof_stages.summary: The lecture defines trivial Chow groups, sets up smooth complete intersections inside a Chow-trivial smooth projective variety X, and uses Lefschetz and hard Lefschetz to split the cohomology into an algebraic ambient part and the vanishing part.

proof_stages.label: Hodge coniveau and the generalized conjectures
proof_stages.summary: The Hodge coniveau of the vanishing Hodge structure is introduced, its computation is recalled via Griffiths residues and reduction to hypersurfaces, and the generalized Hodge/geometric-coniveau and generalized Bloch injectivity predictions are formulated.

proof_stages.label: Conditional Bloch-from-coniveau theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is stated: for very general complete intersections in a Chow-trivial X, geometric coniveau at least c of the vanishing cohomology implies injectivity of the cycle class map in Chow degrees below c, subject to a weak standard-conjecture input.

proof_stages.label: Support-to-cycle conjecture N
proof_stages.summary: A concrete conjecture N is formulated saying that a cycle class supported on a closed subset is represented by a cycle supported there, and it is explained as a consequence of mixed Hodge theory plus either the Hodge conjecture or the Lefschetz standard conjecture.

proof_stages.label: Corrected diagonal supported on Y×Y
proof_stages.summary: The diagonal of a fiber is decomposed into ambient and vanishing projectors, the corrected diagonal is formed, and conjecture N turns the geometric-coniveau support condition into a cycle supported on Y×Y with the same cohomology class.

proof_stages.label: Spreading over the universal family
proof_stages.summary: Using the simple universal complete-intersection family over the parameter space and a Hilbert-scheme spreading argument, the fiberwise supported cycles over very general points are promoted to a global supported cycle over the original base, with rational coefficients after descent from finite covers.

proof_stages.label: From fiberwise cohomology to global Chow equality
proof_stages.summary: The fiberwise cohomological equality is corrected to a global equality on the self-fiber product via Leray and Lefschetz, and the trivial Chow groups of that total space convert this cohomological equality into an equality in Chow.

proof_stages.label: Decomposition-of-diagonal conclusion
proof_stages.summary: Restricting the global Chow equality back to a fiber gives a decomposition of the corrected diagonal into an ambient term and a term supported on Y×Y, which by the standard diagonal-decomposition argument yields the desired injectivity of cycle class maps below c.

examples.name: Projective-space complete intersections
examples.note: Taking X to be projective space and the line bundles to be O(d_i) makes the Hodge-coniveau computation concrete in terms of homogeneous equations of degrees d_i.
examples.engagement_reason: he identifies the projective-space case as complete intersections cut out by homogeneous polynomials and states a computability rule, but does not work through the general complete-intersection computation

examples.name: Hypersurface residue computation
examples.note: For a degree-d hypersurface in projective space, Griffiths' residue description shows how the numerical coniveau estimate comes from the impossibility of a numerator polynomial of negative degree.
examples.engagement_reason: he explicitly runs Griffiths’ residue description for a hypersurface, writes the residue form and degree condition, and derives the vanishing from the degree becoming negative

passes.reason: Griffiths residues for a smooth hypersurface give the standard Hodge-filtration computation, not a failure case

examples.name: Fano hypersurfaces in the c=1 case
examples.note: The condition n+1 >= d is identified with the hypersurface being Fano, where rational connectedness and abundance of rational curves explain the known CH_0 conclusion.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies the c=1 hypersurface estimate to get the inequality n+1 ≥ d, identifies this with the Fano condition, and uses rational connectedness/rational curves to discuss CH0
